---
title: "Developing rieszboost"
---

## Repo layout

```
rieszboost/
├── python/
│   ├── rieszboost/              # the library
│   │   ├── __init__.py            # public API surface, lazy imports
│   │   ├── estimator.py           # RieszBooster class (sklearn BaseEstimator)
│   │   ├── estimand.py            # ATE, ATT, TSM, AdditiveShift,
│   │   │                          # LocalShift, StochasticIntervention, Estimand
│   │   ├── augmentation.py        # build_augmented + AugmentedDataset
│   │   ├── losses.py              # LossSpec protocol, SquaredLoss, KLLoss
│   │   ├── diagnostics.py         # diagnose() + Diagnostics dataclass
│   │   ├── tracer.py              # LinearForm, Tracer, trace
│   │   └── backends/              # swappable plugin architecture
│   │       ├── base.py              # Backend, Predictor, FitResult protocols
│   │       ├── xgboost.py           # XGBoostBackend (default)
│   │       └── sklearn.py           # SklearnBackend (any sklearn regressor)
│   ├── tests/                   # pytest suite
│   └── pyproject.toml
├── r/rieszboost/               # R6 wrapper via reticulate
├── examples/                   # Lee-Schuler reproductions
├── docs/                       # this user guide (Quarto website)
├── .github/workflows/docs.yml  # CI: render + deploy on push to main
├── .githooks/pre-commit        # enforces docs-stay-in-sync rule
├── README.md
└── CLAUDE.md                   # implementation-side notes
```

## Run tests

```sh
# Python (51 tests at the time of writing)
.venv/bin/python -m pytest python/tests -v

# R (parity test included)
Rscript -e '
  Sys.setenv(RETICULATE_PYTHON = file.path(getwd(), ".venv/bin/python"))
  pkgload::load_all("r/rieszboost")
  testthat::test_dir("r/rieszboost/tests/testthat")
'
```

`xgboost` requires `libomp` on macOS — `brew install libomp` once.

## Tracer, briefly

The user writes `m(z, alpha)` opaquely with `+`, `-`, scalar `*`, and calls to `alpha(...)`. The library passes a `Tracer` in as `alpha`; each call returns a single-term `LinearForm`; the user's `m` composes these via Python operator overloading. The result is a `LinearForm` whose `.as_pairs()` returns `[(coef, point), ...]` — exactly what `build_augmented` needs to construct the augmented dataset. Anything outside the linear-form algebra (squaring, integrals, constants) raises immediately at trace time, which keeps users on the rails of the fast path. See [`python/rieszboost/tracer.py`](https://github.com/alejandroschuler/rieszboost/blob/main/python/rieszboost/tracer.py) for the full implementation (~130 lines, no external deps).

## Adding a new estimand factory

A factory is a function that returns an `Estimand`. Pattern from `estimand.py`:

```python
def MyEstimand(param, treatment="a", covariates=("x",)) -> Estimand:
    cov = tuple(covariates)
    def m(z, alpha):
        x_kwargs = {k: z[k] for k in cov}
        # ...build a LinearForm using +, -, scalar*, alpha(...)...
        return alpha(**{treatment: param, **x_kwargs})
    return Estimand(feature_keys=(treatment, *cov), m=m, name=f"MyEstimand({param})")
```

Then:

1. Export from `python/rieszboost/__init__.py` (add to the imports and to `__all__`).
2. Add an R wrapper in `r/rieszboost/R/rieszboost.R` (one-liner that forwards to the Python factory) and `@export` it.
3. Update `docs/estimands.qmd` with a row in the built-ins table and a runnable example in both languages.
4. Add a test under `python/tests/`.

The pre-commit hook will block step (1)/(2) if you forget step (3).

## Adding a new `LossSpec`

Implement the seven-method `LossSpec` protocol from `losses.py`. `SquaredLoss` (35 lines) is the simplest template; `KLLoss` (45 lines) shows how to handle a non-identity link with overflow protection.

1. Add the class to `python/rieszboost/losses.py`.
2. Export from `__init__.py`.
3. Add a one-liner R wrapper.
4. Add a runnable example to `docs/backends-and-losses.qmd`.
5. Test with `pytest python/tests/test_bregman.py`.

## Adding a new backend

Implement the `Backend` protocol in `python/rieszboost/backends/base.py` — one method, `fit_augmented(...)`, returning a `FitResult` whose `predictor` implements `predict_eta` and `predict_alpha`. `XGBoostBackend` and `SklearnBackend` are the two existing templates; the lightgbm backend is on the roadmap and will follow the xgboost template closely.

The backend doesn't need to know about the estimand or the tracer — it sees a finished `AugmentedDataset` and a `LossSpec` and returns a fitted predictor.

## Living-doc rule

Two related rules in CLAUDE.md:

1. `README.md` is the public-facing contract. If a change to the library makes any line of the README false, the change is not done until the README is fixed in the same edit.
2. The user guide under `docs/` is the deeper reference. The same rule applies — public-API changes update the relevant `.qmd` page (in **both** the Python and R tabs where the page is bilingual) in the same commit.

This is enforced by the pre-commit hook at `.githooks/pre-commit`. Activate per clone:

```sh
git config core.hooksPath .githooks
```

The hook looks at staged files; if any of these are modified:

- `python/rieszboost/{__init__,estimator,estimand,augmentation,losses,diagnostics,tracer}.py`
- `python/rieszboost/backends/*.py`
- `r/rieszboost/R/*.R`

then at least one of these must also be staged:

- `docs/*.qmd`
- `docs/_quarto.yml`
- `README.md`

Otherwise the commit is rejected with a clear error pointing at the unmatched files.

For genuinely doc-irrelevant changes (internal refactor that doesn't touch any signature, test additions, comment fixes), bypass with `git commit --no-verify`. That's the explicit escape hatch — there's no shame in using it when the situation calls for it.

## Local docs render

```sh
quarto preview docs/        # live-reload at http://localhost:port
quarto render docs/         # one-shot build to docs/_site/
open docs/_site/index.html
```

Renders take ~30s with `_freeze/` cache (which IS committed to git), ~3 minutes cold. Editing a single `.qmd` only re-renders that page.

## CI

The workflow at [`.github/workflows/docs.yml`](https://github.com/alejandroschuler/rieszboost/blob/main/.github/workflows/docs.yml) runs on push to `main` when any of `docs/`, `python/`, `examples/`, or the workflow itself changes. It:

1. Checks out the repo (which restores the `_freeze/` cache).
2. Installs Python 3.11, then `pip install -e python/` plus `matplotlib`.
3. Installs R + `knitr`, `rmarkdown`, `reticulate`.
4. Sets up Quarto.
5. Renders `docs/` (re-executing only chapters whose source changed).
6. Publishes the rendered `_site/` to the `gh-pages` branch.

The published site lives at `https://alejandroschuler.github.io/rieszboost/`.
